coredata.dc:title: Aloe-vera conjugated natural Carbon Quantum dots as Bio-enhancers to accelerate the repair of chronic wounds
coredata.dc:creator: P.K, Praseetha
A, Nijam Mohaideen
B.V, Vibala
K, Sreedevy
S, Vijayakumar
coredata.dc:description: Carbon Quantum dots (CQDs) when combined with the traditional wound healing agent, Aloe vera gel can offer a feasible solution to the medical challenge posed by chronic wounds that has been unanswered for decades. CQDs are synthesized from Beet root extract through microwave method and further characterized with UV–vis spectroscopy, FTIR, Photoluminescence and TEM- EDAX to reveal the ultra-small sized (0.5–5 nm) uniformly distributed Carbon Quantum dots that were capable to bind with proteins. The CQDs exhibited antibacterial properties, tissue biocompatibility, cell necrosis and promoted angiogenesis. The wound healing mechanisms are confirmed through scratch-wound recovery assay, the in vitro method and zebra fish groups were utilized to assess the regenerative properties in vivo. The nanoconjugate, CQD with the Aloe-vera, when tropically applied, the healing procedures are fastened. The charge imposed by the CQDs positively serves as bio-enhancers to promote the restorative processes in the wounded region.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Quantum dots
Wound closure
Angiogenesis
Biocompatible

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Material and methods
Synthesis and characterization of CQD conjugated aloe-vera gel
Analyzing the constituents in the Aloe vera (Aloe barbadensis)
Anti- microbial activity of the CQD conjugated Aloe-vera gel
Biocompatibility and cytotoxicity studies on the nanoconjugate in animal cell cultures
In vivo toxicity of the nanoconjugate against zebra fish cultures
Wound closure studies: in vitro, in vivo
Statistical examination
Results
Synthesis and characterization of CQD and its conjugate
Biocompatibility and cytotoxicity studies of the CQDs and its nanoconjugates in animal cell cultures
In vivo toxicity of the nanoconjugate against zebra fish cultures
Wound closure studies: in vitro, in vivo
Wound healing–in vivo
Discussion
Conclusions
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgements
References

The authors thank the management of Noorul Islam Centre for Higher Education, Kumaracoil for the full-fledged support provided to execute and complete the research work successfully.

The authors are highly grateful to the funding provided by DST ( SR/FST/ET-1/2017/87 ) and TNSCST/STP-PRG/AR/2018-2019 . The authors thank the management of Noorul Islam Centre for Higher Education, Kumaracoil for the full-fledged support provided to execute and complete the research work successfully.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) UV–vis spectrum of carbon quantum dots. b) UV–vis spectrum of conjugated CQDs.
(a) FTIR spectrum of carbon quantum dots. (b) FTIR spectrum of conjugated CQDs.
a) XRD spectrum of carbon quantum dots. b) XRD spectrum of conjugated CQDs.
a) PL spectrum of carbon quantum dots. b) PL spectrum of aloe conjugated CQDs.

a) TEM analysis of Carbon quantum dots (b) TEM of aloe emodin conjugated CQDs (c) EDAX analysis of CQD nanoconjugates (d) SAED pattern of the nanocrystals Analyzing the constituents in the Aloe vera (Aloe barbadensis).

a) Antimicrobial activity against Bacillus subtilis strains for different samples (b) Antimicrobial activity against Staphylococcus aureus strains for different samples (c) Antimicrobial activity against Escherichia coli strains for different samples.

(a) Tabulation of growth inhibition by the CQDs, aloe and positive control (b) Cell viability recorded by MTT assay for MCF-7 cell lines (c) Cell viability recorded by MTT assay for fR-2 cell lines (d) Microscopic images of MCF-7 cells incubated with CQDs conjugated to Aloe-emodin (e) Microscopic images after treating the MCF-7 cells with CQDs (f) Microscopic images of Negative control- MCF-7 cell lines.
(a) Zebra fish Embryos at 6 h (b) Zebra fish Embryos at 24 h.

a. Scratch wound recovery assay after treatment with CQDs at different time intervals (Optical microscopy images at 10X). b. Scratch wound recovery assay after treatment with CQDs conjugated to Aloe vera gel (Optical microscopy images at 10X).

a) Visual images of the wound receding through the healing process during 0-14 days (Wounded zones are marked in red for better visualization). b) Graphical representation of time taken plotted against wound size for the healing procedure.
Excitation and emission peaks of the Photoluminescence Spectrum.
List of compounds identified at various retention times from Aloe barbadensis.
Antibacterial activity noted through the recorded zone of inhibitions.
Hemolytic percentage for the CQD nano-conjugate under various concentrations.
Toxicology analysis through embryo viability and heart beat rate.

head.ce:title: Aloe-vera conjugated natural Carbon Quantum dots as Bio-enhancers to accelerate the repair of chronic wounds

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanotechnology, Noorul Islam Centre for Higher Education, Kumaracoil, Tamil Nadu, 629180, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Noorul Islam Centre for Higher Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanotechnology, Noorul Islam Centre for Higher Education, Kumaracoil, Tamil Nadu, 628190, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Computational Phytochemistry Laboratory, PG and Research Department of Botany, A.V.V.M Sri Pushpam College, Poondi, Thanjavur, (Affiliated to Bharathidasan University), Tamil Nadu, 613503, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: PG and Research Department of Botany

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Computational Phytochemistry Laboratory PG and Research Department of Botany A.V.V.M Sri Pushpam College, Poondi, Thanjavur, (Affiliations to Bharathidasan University)Tamil Nadu, India. 613503

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon Quantum dots (CQDs) when combined with the traditional wound healing agent, Aloe vera gel can offer a feasible solution to the medical challenge posed by chronic wounds that has been unanswered for decades. CQDs are synthesized from Beet root extract through microwave method and further characterized with UV–vis spectroscopy, FTIR, Photoluminescence and TEM- EDAX to reveal the ultra-small sized (0.5–5 nm) uniformly distributed Carbon Quantum dots that were capable to bind with proteins. The CQDs exhibited antibacterial properties, tissue biocompatibility, cell necrosis and promoted angiogenesis. The wound healing mechanisms are confirmed through scratch-wound recovery assay, the in vitro method and zebra fish groups were utilized to assess the regenerative properties in vivo. The nanoconjugate, CQD with the Aloe-vera, when tropically applied, the healing procedures are fastened. The charge imposed by the CQDs positively serves as bio-enhancers to promote the restorative processes in the wounded region.

ce:section.ce:para: Affordable healthcare for people in rural areas is a global issue currently and immediate solutions are required for the same. Chronic wounds resulted due to diabetes and other ulcers are tough to treat and hence require multiple antibiotics and anti-inflammatory agents even to bring in minimal levels of cure (Gaofeng et al., 2020).The research involving wound healing is on for decades and hence consumes an ordeal source of healthcare throughout the world (Huan-Huan et al., 2019).

Fluorescent CQDs are promising tools which could be synthesized by natural sources of antibacterial acids such as citric acid or carbohydrates and be used to construct assemblies of non- toxic and powerful wound healing agents (Saikumar et al., 2021). Microwave mediated synthesis of CQDs have an advantage of possessing charges on the surface of CQDs. Passivized CQDs have been found to possess negative charges on their surface that enables better affinity for particle binding leading to cell penetration. XPS and FTIR characterization studies have further proven the presence of –OH, COOH, NH2 and N- on the surface (Manioudakis et al., 2018). The surface properties are dictated by the functional groups like carboxyl, thiol, amines and phosphates. The CQDs when functionalized with herbal sources have been demonstrated as one of the major reasons for its high antibacterial and wound healing property reported. This novel property could be used for targeting pathogens through their ROS activated oxidative stress mechanism and employed to test their effectiveness in wound healing properties through direct contact with skin. The promising non-toxic, antibacterial and biocompatibility offered by Carbon Quantum dots do provide possible solutions to answer the issue of life threatening diseases in India and globally against deep rooted wounds.

Aloe vera gel is a popular, traditional wound healing agent and is preferred currently by many of the dermatologists over the conventional chemical therapeutics as they increase the collagen content, improves cross linking and are ecofriendly (Sanchez et al., 2020). Amalgamating the properties of CQDs together with Aloe vera can result in the production of compounds that stimulate the cells for better penetration and therapy. Aloe-emodin, the major constituent of Aloe vera gel is responsible for the wound healing activities, especially for burn wounds. The anti- inflammatory and anti- microbial compounds, Aloe-emodin, aloin and acemannan present in the aloe gel add up to the curing and regeneration activities by the CQDs (Chang et al., 2020). The negative charge remained on the surface of the CQDs obtained by microwave method promoted the easy entry of cell membrane thus acting as bio-enhancers for the anti- inflammation and anti- bacterial effects leading to wound closure and secondary infections at a very early stage (Sargin et al., 2019).

All the chemicals required for the synthesis experiments and assays were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The natural substrates required for the synthesis and assays was procured from the local market (Beta vulgaris and Aloe barbadensis) and identified using the Rapinot herbarium, Trichy. The microbial strains were purchased from ATCC culture collection, Pune, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQD conjugated aloe-vera gel
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots were synthesized using microwave assisted one step synthesis using an oven of 350 W. 20 g of Beta vulgaris (beetroot) extract was taken and kept in the microwave for 20 min. The dried part was collected separately and dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water and stirred using the magnetic stirrer for 10−20 min. The obtained product was cooled and the products (crude) were dialyzed with deionized water (500 mL) for 24 h and lyophilized for further characterization. Purified and powdered CQDs were conjugated with aloe-emodin obtained from Aloe vera gel (Aloe barbadensis) at different concentrations using a magnetic stirrer at 40 ° C for one hour and the pure gel was collected after centrifugation. The encapsulation efficiency or loading capacity of the CQDs to the gel was measured by checking the intensity of fluorescence by CQDs. The pure CQDs and aloe-vera conjugated CQDs were subjected to characterization techniques to observe the optical, structural and surface characteristics through UV–vis Spectroscopy, FTIR, XRD, Photoluminescence and Transmission Electron Microscopy with Energy Dispersive spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyzing the constituents in the Aloe vera (Aloe barbadensis)
ce:section.ce:para: GC–MS (GC Clarus 500 Varian, USA) was performed for the extract of Aloe barbadensis and were analyzed for the presence of various bioactive products. The auto sampler AOC-20I and gas chromatograph were interfaced to a mass spectrophotometer instrument utilizing the supporting conditions: Column Elite-1 combined silica capillary column with 30 mm × 0.25 mm I.D × 1 μ M df, which is made out of 100 % Dimethyl polysiloxane, programming in impact mode of electron at 70 eV; helium (99.999 %) was utilized as transporter gas at a steady stream of 1 mL/min and 0.5 μ l of sample was injected in column (part proportion of10:1). Injector temperature was maintained at 250 °C and temperature of iron was maintained at 280 °C. The temperature of the oven was operated at 110 °C (isothermal for 2 min), with an expansion of 10 °C/min, to 200 °C, 5 °C/min to 280 °C, finishing with 9 min isothermally at 280 °C. Mass spectra were recorded at 70 eV; following interval scans of 0.5 s and fragments from 45 to 450 Da.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti- microbial activity of the CQD conjugated Aloe-vera gel
ce:section.ce:para: With an advantage of being synthesized by one step method, the ultra-small sized Carbon Quantum Dots with potential positive charged surfaces were used for the antibacterial studies. The cell membrane interaction and penetration activities of Quantum dots are influenced largely by the particle size. The Minimal Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) values were calculated by standard dilution method with different bacterial strains including pathogenic strains with multidrug resistance. The pathogens which cause secondary infections on wounds were preferred for the study. Both the Gram Negative and Positive strains were utilized for the study. Escherichia coli from the Gram-negative category; Bacillus subtilis and Staphylococcus aureus from the gram-positive category were used for the analysis. Six samples were used for the study: CQD, Aloe vera, CQD + Aloe vera, a positive (Streptomycin) and negative control with no added antibiotic or samples to it. The diameter of the inhibition zones was measured to assess the anti-bacterial effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and cytotoxicity studies on the nanoconjugate in animal cell cultures
ce:section.ce:para:
The cytotoxicity of the CQDs conjugated with aloe-vera is to be assessed so that it reveals the importance of the herbal based CQD and Aloe vera in destroying the diseased cells that favour tissue necrosis. The test compounds were tested against human breast cancer cell line namely MCF-7 and against normal breast cell line fR-2. All the cell lines used for the study were purchased from European Collection of Cell Culture (ECACC). 15 mg of MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) completely dissolved in 3 mL PBS solution and was filter sterilized. The sample contents were placed into wells and were incubated for 24 h. After incubation, the sample contents were removed and 30 μL of MTT solution was added to the test samples as well as the control wells. After gently shaking the plates, they were kept in a humidified 5% carbon dioxide incubator for 4 h at 37 °C. After incubation MTT solution was removed and DMSO was added (100 μl/well) in the micro titer plate and further shaken at room temperature for 1 h. A purple colored complex was observed and color darkened over the 1 h incubation period. After the 1 h incubation, Formosan precipitate was dissolved by pipetting each well until the precipitate vanished. Absorbance was taken at 570 nm using micro plate reader. The mean Optical Density (OD570) of the percentage of controls was assumed 100 % viability. Results were tabulated and calculated. The Half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) values were calculated using Graph Pad Prism software (Version 5.0).The percentage of cell viability was calculated using below formula
P e r c e n t a g e
o f
v i a b i l i t y
%
=
M e a n
O D
s a m p l e s
M e a n
o f
c o n t r o l
g r o u p
X
100

The Haemolysis assay was performed to assess the biocompatibility of the material to blood and the vascular tissues. Initially, 5 mL of blood was collected from volunteers in tubes which contained EDTA. This was done to prevent coagulant formation and was then centrifuged for 10 min at 1000 rpm, 40 °C. The plasma was carefully removed, by aspiration and using a pipette, the white Buffy layer was removed. Additionally, the erythrocytes were then washed three times with PBS solution having a pH 7.4 for 5 min. This was then used within 6 h for the analysis.50 μL of 10 dilutions of erythrocyte suspension (1:9) was mixed with 100 μL of the test samples. The negative control used was 100 μL of 1X Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS) and the positive control taken was 100 μL of 1 % Sodium Dodecyl Sulphate (SDS). The reaction mixture was then incubated for 60 min at 37 °C water bath. The volume was made upto 1 mL by adding 850 μL of 1X Phosphate Buffer (PB). It was finally centrifuged at 300 rpm for 3 min. The supernatant which contained the resulting haemoglobin was measured at 540 nm by a micro plate reader and 630 nm as reference was taken in order to determine the haemoglobin concentration. The percentage for haemolysis inhibition was calculated using the following equation
H e m o l y s i s
i n h i b i t i o n
%
=
O D
o f
t e s t
c o n c e n t r a t i o n
O D
o f
1 %
S D S
X
100

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo toxicity of the nanoconjugate against zebra fish cultures
ce:section.ce:para: Zebra fish (Brachydanio rerio) which are extensively used as model organisms in advanced research due to their similarities to human development and diseases were utilized for the current study. Initially, the zebra fish embryos were analyzed for their tolerance towards the nanomaterial. The mortality and heart beat rate was analyzed using standard methods. A total of five tanks were used for the study. Each of the tanks was maintained with 100 embryos. The first group was treated with CQD samples, second with the Aloe vera gel and the third with the nanoconjugate of Aloe vera and CQDs together. One positive control with the cipladinand a negative control tank with no added samples or nanomaterial were also maintained throughout the experimentation.Heart beat rate of the Embryos was analyzed by using the CQD and Aloe vera samples prepared. Heart beat rate was tested 72 h post fertilization. Heart beat rate was measured every 2 h, 6 h and 24 h interval. The survival percentage of the embryos post hatching was analyzed in every group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wound closure studies: in vitro, in vivo
ce:section.ce:para:
The cells at a density of 5 × 105were seeded in to each well of a 24- well plate and incubated with complete medium at 37 °C and 5% CO2. After incubation, the monolayer cells were scrapped horizontally with a sterile pipette tip. The wells were washed thoroughly with PBS to remove any debris. The cells were treated with 50ug/mL concentration of each sample and used media was replaced with serum free DMEM. Cells without treatment and those treated with DMSO served as controls. Standard drug cipladine treatment at 5ug/mL was also included. The scratch done was photographed at different time intervals (0, 3, 6, 16, 20, 24, 32, 48 h) using phase contrast microscope. Imaging software by National Institute of Health (NIH Image-J) was then employed to determine the migration rates. A decrease in the scratch area indicated the migration of the cells. The percentage wound closure was calculated from the area obtained using the equation:
Wound closure (%) = (Area at 0 h − Area at X hour) × 100
Area at 0 h, Where X = 3, 6, 16, 20,24,32,48 h

The results indicated that the wound closure was higher and speedy in the cells treated with CQDs conjugated with Aloe vera than CQDs or Aloe vera treated alone. The curing rate and efficiency was found to be better when compared to the control and standard drugs.

For in vivo studies, the fish tanks were filled with clean water and the fishes were acclimatized with good aeration overnight. The disinfected fishes were introduced to the respective tanks where the temperature and other physiological conditions were monitored. Five different groups of fishes were maintained: Tank A to E, where A is the positive control with added cipladin and E is the negative control with no drug added. Fish groups were anesthetized with chloroform and a sharp cut of around 2.5 mm was made in the tail region. Groups B (CQD + AVG), C(CQD) and D (AVG) are consecutively added with 1 mL of the samples every 48 h after cleaning and collecting the feces and left over food. The wound size was monitored every 48 h through 2000X digital microscope, was photographed and the wound size was measured with image-J simultaneously. The death rate was recorded individually.
All the tests were performed three times. The results were shown as mean ± standard errors, and analyzed through one-way variant analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQD and its conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: One step microwave method was employed to synthesize Carbon Quantum dots from fresh beet root extracts (aqueous). The purified CQDs through centrifugation/dialysis were subjected to characterization in order to assess the physical, optical and structural properties. The UV–vis absorption spectroscopy of the first sample dissolved in water shows an absorption peak whose at 280 nm, indicating the formation of carbon quantum dots. The absorption peak corresponds to the transistion π-π of C = C. The conjugated CQDs with aloe emodin displays a double peak both at 250 and 320 nm (Fig. 1 a and b). The shift in the absorption has been visualized due to the perfect conjugation of the compound to the respective biomolecule.

FTIR spectrum of CQDs peak at 1427 cm−1 may be due to CO stretching,1560 cm −1 due to CC unsaturated alkynes. While 2329cm−1 and 2364 cm−1 corresponds to NH stretching amine groups. FTIR spectrum of CQDs + Aloe-emodin peak at 1522cm−1 may be due to CC stretching of unsaturated alkenes,1733 cm−1 due to CO bend primary aldehyde carboxyl compound. While 2336 cm-1 amine nitrogen group, 2845 cm−1 and 2917 cm−1 aliphatic methyl group and 3236 cm−1 corresponds to CH unsaturated alkene (Fig. 2 a and b).

The XRD pattern of Beta vulgaris is shown in Fig. 3 a and b. The broad reflection seen at 2 theta = 22.81 degree is attributed to the presence of CQDs. The CQDs conjugated to aloe emodin was found to produce the XRD peaks at 23.703degree corresponding to (002) plane. From the Scherrer equation the average crystallite size of CQDs was found to be 1.16992 nm and of the nanoconjugated crystals were of 6 nm (Huang et al., 2019).

Fig. 4 a and b and Table 1 show the PL spectra of the carbon quantum dots individually and Carbon Dots with aloe emodin samples when excited at different wavelengths. The excitation wavelengths and the emission wavelengths were given in their respective tables. The maximum fluorescence was observed to be 530 nm for CQDs at the excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The Photo luminescent emissions were found to decrease when combined with aloe emodin due to quenching property of the CQDs. Fluorescence is one of the properties of CQDs, and the ranges of excitation and emission wavelengths show the presence of CQDs in the sample and their proper conjugation with the biomolecule aloe-emodin.

Using the TEM images the average particle size of CQDs were found to be 2.5 nm which is the average size of carbon quantum dots which indicates the presence of carbon quantum dots in the sample. The size ranges are perfectly suitable for cell penetration and membrane intercalation for further disruption of bacterial peptidoglycans. The aloe emodin conjugated CQDs were found to exhibit a size range of 5−10 nm on an average. The distribution pattern of the dots were found to be even and the particles also displayed a perfect spherical structure when present individually (Fig. 5 a–d). EDX analysis revealed the presence of carbon elements to the maximum level with few minor peaks that originated from the active beet root substrates (Elsohly et al., 2007).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and cytotoxicity studies of the CQDs and its nanoconjugates in animal cell cultures
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the biocompatibility of Carbon Quantum Dots, an in vitro monitoring of cytotoxicity and hemolysis was performed. The uptake of CQDs by the cells revealed insignificant cytotoxicity towards the cell lines (Table 4 ).

Cytotoxicity analysis was performed for both CQDs and Aloe-emodin conjugated CQDs through MTT assay (Fig. 8 a–f). The animal cell cultures of Breast cancer and normal cells were utilized: MCF-7 (Breast cancer) and fR-2 (Breast Normal). Doxorubicin was used as the positive control. The CQDs, Aloe-CQDs and Doxorubicin were supplied to the cell lines at 4 different concentrations of 0.1, 1, 10 and 100 μg/mL. It was noticed that the cytotoxic responses increased with increase in the concentration of the samples. Aloe-emodin conjugated CQDs exhibited a maximum cytotoxicity of 80.3 % against MCF-7 Breast cancer cell lines which was almost equivalent to the chemotherapeutic drug, doxorubicin. CQDs did exhibit a growth inhibition percentage of 51.2 against the breast cancer cell lines. But inhibited normal cell lines too at 68.8 % for higher concentrations (Ding et al., 2018). It was observed that there is little cytotoxicity in both the CQDs and the aloe-emodin conjugated CQDs as evidenced in the microscopic visualization. Both the negative control and the hybrid scaffold showed no reduction in cell metabolic activity. All the experiments were performed in triplicates and the mean value was considered as the result. This is consistent with previous studies which suggested that aloe-emodin promotes cell adhesion and growth and CQDs do not interfere with the basic bio-molecular proliferation and migration. The size of the particles and the functional groups present on them favors and contributes to the reproduction and longevity of cells. The revealed results prove that the conjugates present excellent biocompatibility and are well suited for animal/human administration to estimate the wound healing functions both for cell lines and in vivo conditions. The results are in accordance with the results obtained earlier (Wu et al., 2006).

Haemolysis assay is an important technique to analyse the bio safety levels of a newly synthesized nanomaterials which would deal in with intra cellular and tissue related pharmaceutical applications. Haemolytic assays were performed because compounds possessing potent biological activity may not be useful in tissue regeneration if they possess haemolytic effect. In addition, these data reveals some information about the mechanism of cytotoxicity. The hemolysis induced by CQDs and its conjugates in erythrocytes was concentration-dependent. Hemolytic activities also proved to be negligible and were found to be harmless to the Red blood cells even at higher concentrations. Haemolytic activity of the hybrid scaffold is shown in Table 5 . Each concentration shows the mean of haemolysis percentage repeated in three experiments. The haemolysis induced by extracts in red blood cells was concentration-dependent but the hybrid scaffold showed lower haemolytic effect on human red blood cell on all concentrations tested. None of the concentration test of hybrid scaffold possessed any haemolytic activity against human erythrocytes. The effect of various concentrations on blood erythrocyte membrane showed no changes in haemolytic activity when concentration varied from 50 μg to 500 μg per milliliter. The haemolytic percentages of the hybrid scaffold 1.06 %, 2.29 %, 3.53 %, 4.54 and 5.97 % were obtained for a dose of 6.25 μg/mL, 12.5 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL respectively. The positive reference is 100 % haemolytic, and the negative reference is 5.91 %. The nanoconjugate has haemolysis percentage less than 6% which indicates that this biomaterial scaffolds exhibit good biocompatibility and may be suitable for biomedical applications. These results are comparable with the previous study which produced similar results and values (Tayline et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo toxicity of the nanoconjugate against zebra fish cultures
ce:section.ce:para: Zebra fish (Brachydanio rerio) were chosen for the in vivo studies, especially to test the toxicity of CQDs and aloe-emodin conjugated CQDs (Fig. 9 ). The embryo hatching and mortality test were done to check the overall non- toxic nature of the nanoconjugate before performing the wound healing studies. Mortality test has been conducted in zebra fish embryos (100 numbers) by introducing the samples to the culture tank. Embryos for mortality test are tested on the same day of 3−4 h post fertilization. Each individual group was monitored with 100 embryos each and the mortality was tested for 2 h, 6 h and 24 h. Embryo heart rate test were performed on the same day at 72 h post fertilization. Heart beat rate were recorded for 2 h, 6 h and 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wound closure studies: in vitro, in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro studies of scratch-wound recovery assay were performed (Fig. 10 ). A total of 2.5 × 104 cells were added with antibiotics and were treated with the CQDs and CQD Aloe vera gel combinations. The incubation was done at 37 °C for 48 h to produce monolayers. Cell free scratches were created in the monolayers with a wound maker. The free cells were washed with PBS. The scratch/ wound created and the wound closures were observed with a fluorescent microscope and were compared with the control. The changes in the wound and rate of wound closures were recorded uniformly at 0–48 h at equal intervals and the synergistic effect of Aloe vera and CQDs were compared. CQD + Aloe vera gel conjugate was observed to produce a faster wound healing rate compared to CQD alone and was easy to heal than the control assays (Fig. 10a and b). Cell death percentage was observed to be less in the fibroblast cells treated with CQD and Aloe vera gel.

The samples, CQD and CQD conjugated with Aloe vera gel were used for the wound healing studies using zebra fish for in vivo analyses (Fig. 11 a and b). Five tanks, each with 25 fishes were used for the study. Tank A to E, with the CQD, Aloe vera, CQD + Aloe vera nanoconjugate, positive and negative controls were utilized to measure the speed of wound healing in their tail region. The samples were introduced to the culture tanks at 1% (W/v) of water in which the fishes were grown with water exchange at every two days interval. An average size of 2.5 mm wound was created near the tail fin. The size of the wounds from Day 1 was measured every 48 h using the image-J software, until it was completely closed with the scar alone left behind. The scales were not found to regenerate in the wounded region. The speed of wound healing was recorded to be the maximum, ie., 10 days in CQD + Aloe vera gel conjugate. Aloe vera gel alone heals the wound completely in 11 days, whereas, CQD treated fishes healed at 12 day duration. The fish groups on an average recorded 5 % death throughout the experimentation period.

The current work determines the effect of aloe- emodin and other macromolecules from Aloe vera gel when conjugated to Carbon Quantum Dots synthesized by microwave method performs better than the individual nanomaterial or the biomolecule act individually. Microwave method of synthesizing Carbon Quantum dots was preferred as the polarity determines the interaction between precursors and the solvents and hence hydrophilic, well dispersible, particles with uniform dispersion could be synthesized. The OH and COOH groups on the surfaces contribute to their water dispersibility. The size ranges fall from 0.5 to 5 nm as determined by TEM. When nanoparticles or composite materials are meant for biomedical usage, precise control over their size, shape, pore size and pore geometry are crucial. The pore size, surface area and orientation have proven to play important roles in their biocompatibility and translocation.Nanoparticles could be designed based on this chemo physical properties and bio compatibility (Juqun et al., 2018). CQDs synthesized by microwave method have been reported to bind efficiently to Biomolecules such as DNA, amino acids, nucleotides and proteins at very high concentrations (Zhao et al., 2018).

After the synthesis, the CQDs were conjugated with aloe- emodin for further applications after characterizing the nanomaterials physically, optically and structurally. The current study correlated with the earlier study in which the hybrid gel with Aloe vera and nanoparticles were found to reduce the bacterial count in a short period through Infra- red light induced photo thermal and ROS by releasing aloe-emodin from the Aloe vera gel (Teplicki et al., 2018). Aloe vera had significant stimulatory effects on cell proliferation and migration of both fibroblasts. In vitro analyses of cell proliferation and migration were conducted on normal human primary skin fibroblasts. Cellular uptake and behavior is solely dependent on the diameter of the particles chosen, the cell lines chosen and on the serum protein availability. The particles do have greater impact on cellular functions like cell proliferation, apoptosis, adhesion, migration and formation of cytoskeleton which is due to cellular internalization and increased uptake ratio. The different uptake pathways determine the intracellular fate of the particles used. Current references support the use of CQDs for labeling subcellular organelles as they have proved to penetrate nucleus and organelle membranes (Pan et al., 2019, Binesh et al., 2020).

Zebra fish tissues do exhibit perfect homology to human tissues with respect to their differentiation cycles and metabolic pathways (Saleem Rajaretinam and Kannan, 2018). The CQDs and CQD conjugated with aloe emodin were tested against Zebra fish embryos and found that they do not interfere with their growth processes. At 3−4 h post fertilization, mortality tests were conducted and the heart beat rate analysis was performed on the 72 h post fertilized embryos. Embryos exhibited normal growth patterns and their heart beats were comparable to the control groups. As indicated by few references, aloe emodin plays a major role in wound healing when analyzed individually than aloin. As the results (In vitro and In vivo) indicated that the wound healing was fastened, it is clear that the aloe emodin was the biomolecule conjugated to enable powerful wound closure effects. (Li et al., 2016)

Though nanomedicine has the potential to revolutionize various conventional and therapeutic strategies, the major challenge is the lack of biocompatible particles with uniform distribution and pharmacokinetics for proper cell penetration and excretion. The nanoconjugates have proved to be biocompatible and are capable of improving the bioavailability of the drugs into the targeted location thus functioning as bio-enhancers for aloe emodin and other biomolecules that support wound care.

Nanomaterials, especially, Carbon Quantum dots have proven to be photo-luminescent, photo-catalytic and antimicrobial agents. Aloe vera, the traditional wound healing agent with potential phyto-pharmaceuticals when bio-conjugated to CQDs through this work have demonstrated excellent wound healing rate through zebra fishes as the in vivo model. The fastened rate of wound healing is believed to be possible through antibacterial, angiogenesis, necrotic properties of the CQDs enhanced by the biomolecule, aloe-emodin and the cellular penetration properties of the whole conjugate was accelerated through the CQDs working as bio-enhancers to improve the bioavailability of the biomolecule. These nanomaterials have opened up a new arena for the therapeutic potential of CQDs in the pharmacological research to be scaled up into a commercially viable topical drug.

Praseetha (PK) has design the work, monitor the overall setup including writing the paper, all authors has been helped to finish the paper. Nijam Mohaideen A (NMA) has analyze the angiogenesis. Vibala B.V (VBV) has analyze the biocompatible. Sreedevy K (SK) has monitor the carbon quantum activities. Vijayakumar S (vs) has analyze biochemical constituents.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are highly grateful to the funding provided by DST (SR/FST/ET-1/2017/87) and TNSCST/STP-PRG/AR/2018-2019. The authors thank the management of Noorul Islam Centre for Higher Education, Kumaracoil for the full-fledged support provided to execute and complete the research work successfully.
